speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In this conversation with Roy Glenn and his wife Mabel Richardson Glenn took place at their home in Kendrick on November 18, 1976. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, were you still a little kid?

speaker: Roy Glenn
words: I was one year old. I was one year old when we come to Juliaettas where we landed.

speaker: Roy Glenn
words: We stopped on American Ridge up there for a few years and then we moved over on the Potlatch and I spent all the rest of my years, I'll be 74 years old on New Year's Day and we've been here ever since.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know why it was they picked this part of the country to come to out west?

speaker: Roy Glenn
words: Well, some of my folks earlier, my mother's folks, had come west and settled in American Ridge and so that's why we followed. Yes, it was a better country out here they figured. So they had been back, they'd been out here and then they'd been back to North Carolina and told us about it so we come west.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who were they? Their name?

speaker: Roy Glenn
words: Well, Woody. And then also Martin Thomas used to be the big banker here. He is related to my mother too. So he had been back there.

speaker: Roy Glenn
words: So we all come west and now let's see my mother and then my grandparents and my father of course and who else came home?

speaker: Roy Glenn
words: My mother's brother came too. So we all arrived out here and we've been here ever since and my folks has passed on and yes, my uncles has all passed on and it came.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Has your father been back there in Carolina?

speaker: Roy Glenn
words: No, he worked in the woods most of the time and he was to tell me that he also worked for the government and he worked in a government distillery when they made whiskey in North Carolina. He worked for the government distillery company. Now, she was born on Fakes Ridge. She's been here all her life. Oh, I didn't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was your maiden name?

speaker: Mabel Glenn
words: Richard. Oh, maiden Richard. Yep.

speaker: Mabel Glenn
words: My folks come here from Oregon in 1893 and they landed in the Genesee farm there for a little while. I'd placed here Genesee and they moved off this farm on Fakes Ridge and lived there for a while. Born and raised there until Madam and then went to Texas Ridge.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that land on Fakes Ridge, was that naturally prairie land at the time or was that clear? Do you know where they were?

speaker: Roy Glenn
words: I was wondering what you knew of that land on Fakes Ridge where they were.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it cleared land or if it just never had any trees on it?

speaker: Mabel Glenn
words: Oh, when they came it was they bought it from people by the name of Dew and they had, it was cleared land but it had a great big orchard on and then the rest of it.

speaker: Roy Glenn
words: He's a mean one.

speaker: Roy Glenn
words: Was it prairie land or timber land?

speaker: Roy Glenn
words: Yeah, it was a prairie land.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A lot of that Fakes Ridge was, wasn't it?

speaker: Roy Glenn
words: Yeah, I bet. Busted grass, they always called it. And the American Ridge was too until you get up towards Troy, you know. That was prairie land and that's where old Martin Thomas homesteaded up on the American Ridge. And so he, you could have another 160 if you had a preemption of trees. So he set out five acres of trees to get another 160 with the homestead.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't he about the biggest farmer there for a while?

speaker: Roy Glenn
words: Oh, yeah. Then he was also president of the Kendrick State Bank here for years and years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did he do so well? Was it just he was here earlier or what was it that gave him the leg up?

speaker: Roy Glenn
words: He was just here early to start with. Now that's where he got started.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And your parents owned land at Genesee when they lived there?

speaker: Roy Glenn
words: No, they did and they just. They were kind of looking for a place. Looking for a place, uh -huh.

speaker: Roy Glenn
words: They had a place in Florida that had no place in it. They made a train show.

speaker: Roy Glenn
words: No, no, no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it, did your parents when they come out, did they have much money to start out with or did they just have to start from scratch?

speaker: Roy Glenn
words: Yes, yes. And they, my dad worked on farms and he was also a carpenter and he'd done lots of carpenter work and stuff. That's how he survived for years and years. And of course, us, this family, they had a family of five boys and three girls in the family and they raised all of us. And, um,

speaker: Roy Glenn
words: that's how we survived.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So where was the farm then that you grew up on? Where was it at?

speaker: Roy Glenn
words: It was over here on the Potlatch Ridge and it wasn't too big a place. And we lived there and on a small place then they rented a farm there and a bigger farm and we grew up there and went to school and then after I got grown and all I got thinking about going to army and I didn't go to army so I got to thinking about getting married and I met her and we'd been married for 48 years and we live on Texas Ridge up here, our ranch is up on Texas Ridge. We go back and forth here and the reason we're here is we just had girls and they was in school. This was the school that we had to send them to and there's bad ears getting in and out for the snow, lots of snow up there so we got a hold of this place and they've all been through school and we're here by ourselves now but we still, there was no school here then it was up on the hill in town so they bought this land and we don't know how that land come off of this place but it was before we bought the place that they got this land off of it for the school that's here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well we're on Potlatch Ridge, were you near Leland?

speaker: Roy Glenn
words: We was west of Leland about four miles.

speaker: Roy Glenn
words: Yeah, we was about four miles west of Leland.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what was it like when you were growing up here when you were just a kid?

speaker: Roy Glenn
words: Oh, it was quite different I'll tell you. We had horses and we had all stock on the ranches you know of them and now it's about four miles to Leland. That used to be a little town up there and at one time we just reading in the paper here not long ago that at one time 600 people got their mail at Leland. That was just a small bird but we used to, they had good stores and blacksmith shops. Of course that is one of the great things in the early days was a blacksmith shop in the community because that's the way you kept things rolling. If you had a good smith in the country you know I think most of our work for the machinery and everything and yes and it was all horses at that time. We were just talking a few days ago when they go to plow now they don't make any difference where it's dry or what it is they can plow but them days when you had the horses you had to wait till you had moisture enough that the horses could pull it you know and the plows wasn't made to stay in the ground like they are now so all that was out of way and then our grain now used to be a tram up on the ridge and they'd haul their grain over to this tram and they'd run by a cable and had places of buckets on it and they'd put their grain on these buckets in the sacks and they'd take them all down to the bottom. Well in Juliaetta that was another tram but that run on cars. They had two cars and cables and three of them rode on at a many a time and had three rails but right in the middle of it there's four rails so to pass they just wanted a little wider well they'd potty or grain on that and run it down to Juliaetta and put it on the crane down there they had a warehouse there they didn't haul too much down the grade at that time and oh the old roads was rough and rocky and you know to haul on and if they could get it run down that way why that's the way they got a lot of their grain to the terminal down here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it really dangerous to use them roads for hauling at that time?

speaker: Roy Glenn
words: Oh well no faster than you and it wasn't so much like the other horses then is your old trucks started in and first of them was solid tires you know we had a few of them come in and a few years later then they started to get in small trucks and we just looking at one at Juliaetta well they got one down Juliaetta there now as a relic I believe it was 19 and 22 or something like that it was bought that was about one of the first pneumatic tires they had you know and somebody picked it up out of the junk and they got it they had it running down there the other day and to think that stuff like that and we had one well when we got married like we have the folks in us had one and we an old timer and we got in the 30s and is the same kind you'd haul 27 sacks of grain to town you know and that's pretty good load but it sure has changed that way but everybody had horses and everybody had cows put in there and that is your survival at that time it took about a fourth of 160 to feed the cows and the horses you know what you raised on it so that that's what took up a lot of the stuff which nowadays it's all done by big machinery and that's put on the market you know it and that's that's of course the population is is increased a lot but still that's what makes your overproduction at the present time it's because it's all it's all put on the market instead of like it used to be.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well what kind of crops were would you be getting on potlatch ridge when in those earlier years were they

speaker: Roy Glenn
words: uh was it a good yield then oh yes now one of the things they raised was beans you know we cultivated and hold them you know and that was a good crop but then on this ground you would get a real good crop of grain after beans you would get a lot better crop on bean ground than you would pea ground and your ground was always clean you had to cultivate beans as in rows you know and you'd cultivate them and then you'd also hold them and it took a lot of the young folks's time didn't it?

speaker: Roy Glenn
words: It's always always people kids who want the whole beans you know they get a little money in the summer that is their livelihood for you know for the family to help if the kids you get a little job of holding beans here and there then we'd have to cut them and then they'd have to pile them you know and cut them and then pile them and when they mature then we'd haul them out with the wagons you know and put them in the thrashing machine that took a lot of work there's a lot of work before the thrashing machine come they'd have deal with horses and they'd trap them out and you'd bring those beans in and these horses and have them on a flat surface you see and these horses would go around trap out these beans and then you'd pick up your straw throw out then you'd pick up the beans. Yeah there's a lot of ways at them days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well how come the beans were they better than peas at that time?

speaker: Roy Glenn
words: Well yeah they were we had better ground after beans but here come the peas along and they wasn't so much work to harvest them and then our machinery changed. Here come the combines you know well in the beans most of the time until the last few years you had to haul them and put them in a thrashing machine like they thrashed the early grain in you know they thrashed beans just like the grain only in your machine you said you just about all you done is just slowed your cylinder down so you didn't crack the beans and thrashed them with that same thrashing machine. Yeah they used to it would take 30 and 35 days bean thrashing you know take care of it like on the ridge up there.

speaker: Roy Glenn
words: Well it seemed like that they they went to that more because they didn't have to to have as much work to it and then these year uh combines you know these later ones they could just one man could cut a whole field of peas which the other way you had a lot of of people working and that's why uh I used to think that's why it makes unemployment the kids didn't have anything to do. Yeah oh yeah they just done a lot of that.
